Another "game" sh@ out by Ubisoft. IDK how products like these still manage to be on Steam.

First of all, the game doesn't even work for some people (especially those with Windows 10). I was lucky enough to have it working on my pc but damn, it's still terrible. The settings are pretty much useless because once you launch the campaign, it hovers at around 8-12fps. For reference, my graphics card is a GTX1070 paired with an i7-7700hq so I was expecting a game from 2010 to run without problems.
I even tried to lower the settings and all it did was make the game look worse and gave me 1 extra fps. Definitely not recommended to buy this floating turd of a game because to even play the game, you need to be a computer science major while also learning how to tinker with the game's files which shouldn't even be happening since this is a paid game.

If you are looking at this review right now and wondering if you should pay for this game, the answer is a big fat NO. Games like these don't deserve money and instead deserve to be pirated because there is literally no support from the devs and multiplayer is almost dead anyway.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I like to think im a frugle spender. The first silent hunter game I bought was 'Silent Hunter 3' for around £3-£4. I didnt expect much from an old, cheap game but, My God, was I impressed! for a game that came out when I was 7 its beautiful. I then bought the package for around £25. I imediatly installed 'Silent Hunter 4' and was again blown away!

 Then came 'Silent Hunter 5'. Its not just a game or a Simulator. Its a window into the lives of the crews of the Submarines of World War 2! 
Each of the main crew members has a back story. They all have their own reasons for joining the Kreigsmarine. They are'nt all raving, racist Nazis (bar one). They're all human, and you can really identify with many of them. 

The Graphics are excellent and the gameplay is so realistic it blows my mind. I've had to actually use the maths I spent most of my childhood learning at school to play this game. And that was only optional! 

If you're a history nerd like me and are interested in the human side of WW2 as well as being a Submarine lover then buy this game!

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Ok, there are a lot of bad reviews and there can be difficulties getting this game to run, that's true. But if one follows the advice given on subsim.com and installs the Wolves of Steel MegaMod, this game becomes a gem and feels a lot like SH3 with GWX.

I haven't played it much yet though, I first had to get it to run - one problem was for example, that it didn't start at all at first and Uplay crashed - reinstalling Uplay solved this. I had to make a few changes to the compatibility tab of the game executables (related to DPI, run as admin and windows 7 compatibility mode), but then it worked.

I can recommend it to those familiar with SH3 with GWX (as SH5 with Wolves of Steel installed), but not to those, who are not familiar with playing Silent Hunter,, because the manual of SH5 is ridiculous and Wolves of Steel does as well not have a manual (in contrast to GWX mod with SH3, which has a very extensive manual).

The Wolves of Steel mod is very good though and has lots of options - you can even use an Enigma machine to decode radio transmissions from BdU if you want to. The progression system is changed in pleasant ways, overall I know already that I will spend hundreds of hours with this game - I love SH3 with GWX, but SH5 with Wolves of Steel has advanced features, which might even be better in certain play styles - but it takes some effort to get into it.

This is actually what I did and why my play time is nearly non-existant for now - I watched a couple of videos on Youtube to decide, how much of realism i want. Wolves of Steel has even realistic navigation, where your boat and heading is not on the map at all, you have to figure that out yourself where you are and where you're heading - I like that a lot.

So, yes, can be recommended, but not for those, who do not want to put in the effort to get it to run - it might take some effort to figure that out. And I highly recommend to use Wolves of Steel, otherwise the game play is odd.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
like all silent hunter games.. this is HARD... it is brutal... it is realistic... you will die OFTEN... most submarine crews DIDNT make it. its why they had the respect of both sides.. even after the war, americans still respected and feared german submarine crews. brave and deadly.. and near suicidal insanity. torpedos are NOT the main weapon. this isnt some movie. torps stop a target or slow them enough for you to get away or finish the job with the deck gun. and german gun crews were some of the best in the world. use your wits, your skills, your luckiest of trinkets, and a few well placed prayers and you may live long enough to be one of the best. if luck, fate, the gods, or training are against you, then you will join the merchant ships you sunk in Davy Jones' locker. run silent, run deep, and may the sea have mercy on you.

I have played other silent hunter games. this one basicly includes the features we modded into the earlier ones. great action, dificult foes, and even some history. for a fun game, turn it all the way to easy and enjoy the frag fest.. for the ultimate challenge, turn it to full realistic and see why even some of the best commanders ended their carreers on the bottom in crushed and blown out hulls.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I know, I know ...

The game is busted.  It doesn't work.  Bugs all over the place.  Don't buy it ...

... and so on, and so forth.

However, in years past, I've played this game for hours on end, without any bugs, no CTDs, nothing.  So, how did I do it?

Mods.  Mods are the key to just about every game from Ubisoft (which is not one of my favorite game publishers, but they do have some great ideas).  Specifically, the Wolves of Steel (TWoS), which can be found at SubSim.com.  How good is it?  At times, you would swear that you are the Kapitanleutnant in the movie "Das Boot".  I've devoured that movie (including the 5-hour version of it), and I love TWoS when connected with Silent Hunter 5.

You start the campaign game in the Eastern Baltic, at a port which was Hitler's last bloodless conquest.  War has just broken out against Poland.  At least, it is just about to break out:  the date is August 31, 1939, and there is the Polish Navy to deal with.

After you clear the port, you go on an inspection tour of your "Boot" - short for "Unterseeboot".  You climb down the ladder into the conning tower, and then into the control (while your crew stands watch on the bridge).  You speak to the navigator, the Chief, and go forward through the hatch and speak to your radio operator and sound man.  If you turn around, you'll see your own cabin (a tiny little place with a curtain, your "desk", and your bunk).  Just forward of that location in the ward room, some crew quarters, and the torpedo room.  Going aft, you find some more crew quarters, the tiny galley (which was supposed to prepare food for 53 crewmen), the engine room, and the electric motor room.

If you've ever watched "Das Boot", all of these locations look eerily familiar (and by the way, I've toured the U-555 which is currently on display at the Museum of Science and Industry, in Chicago.

As you continue your operations against the Polish, the British, and all of Germany's enemies (later to include the United States), and you are pursued by aircraft and destroyers, you may feel a surge of adrenaline, increased heart rate ... you feel like you are there.  The game gets harder, as the years pass, and if you survive.

The graphics are beautiful, the gameplay is seamless, and it functions like Ubisoft SHOULD HAVE made it function.

To make it work right, you simply have to get the Wolves of Steel mod.  It's free.  And the basic game costs only $10, so what have you got to lose?

If you have any interest at all, in WW2 naval warfare, and specifically in the U-boat campaign in the Atlantic, you simply have to buy this game.

Strongly recommended ....

After-Action report:
Just two nights ago, I guided my Type VIIb boat into the Gulf of Danzig (using The Wolves of Steel megamod), and found a Polish taskforce consisting of two destroyers.  Although I approached them with decks awash (to lower my profile on the horizon) a lookout must have seen us.  The bottom was 67 meters, so I went to 50 meters depth and went silent.

Depth charges were all over the place, and we got shook up pretty badly, but took no serious damage.  Suddenly, the depth charge attacks ended.  I judged that the destroyers had run out them, and took my boat up to periscope depth.  Carefully edging the attack scope up, till it just broke the surface.  I saw one of the destroyers about 500 meters off.  I made a quick resolution of the attack and sent two torpedoes its way, aiming just below the bridge.  Both hit, and the destroyer went down quickly.

Meanwhile, the other destroyer started to run away, but he was very close, also.  I made a snapshot with tube 3 & 4, and one of them hit.  When tube one was loaded, I hit him, again.  He went down very fast. 

I'm now on my way to my new base, at Kiel.  In the interim, I sank two Polish merchant vessels, one with a torpedo, and one with gunfire.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
This game is so fucking buggy it'll make you cry and give up before you can finnish the first mission. I AM NOT KIDDING!

tl;dr: 0/10 WOULD NOT GET BUGGED AGAIN

long version:

> Got the game
> Got into the sub
> DIDN'T KNOW SHIT
> Got out and went into a fucking steep learning curve
> Got back into the sub
> GOT FIRST MISSION: Sink 100,000 tonnes of merchant ships
> Played for 6 HOURS
> SANK 50,000
> ez gg
> Saved the game
> Got back the next day
> Played for 4 HOURS
> Sank a LOT of ships
> Checks tonnage bar: 50,000
> WTF?
> STARTS LOOKING ON THE FORUMS
> "COMMON BUG, Gotta restart the campaign"
> RESTARTS THE CAMPAIGN
> SAME SHIT HAPPENED AGAIN
> RESTART THE CAMPAIGN [2]
> SAME OLD SHIT


Now, seriously, this game is fucking buggy. The gameplay and graphics are very nice, but this damn mission is on the southwestern shore of England. unless you are prepared to do a 12 hours long session going back and forth through the the damn Norwegian Sea ([i]believe me, it sucks, it takes time and it's boring[/i]), you won't get past the first mission. Also, don't bother trying to leave the game open just so you don't have to load and get bugged, as your game will crash after some time. Also, good luck not having your crew dying of CO2 intoxication just because the game bugged and it thinks you are submerged when you are in fact breathing fresh morning air on the French coast.

Ubisoft don't give a shit about you.

They abandoned the game and will never return. Also, if you want MODS that might fix the problem, don't buy the Steam version.

Move along, this is not the U-Boat Sim you are looking for...

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Apart from a confusing briefing bug that kept me from starting mission 1 after the tutorial, I've had no other glitches so far. Running Windows 10 (don't know why so many people have problems with that, but I don't), GTX 1070, 16 GB RAM, and the game is flippin' goregeous for a title that's almost 8 years old. 

It's a steep learning curve but I've enjoyed climbing it. I never played SH3 or 4 so I can't compare it. Still, I really don't understand all the hate out there - if your rig can't run it, get a refund.  All I can say is that, from my own experience, this title runs fine on modern hardware/OS.

PS - the first mission bug is fixed by completing the tutorial mission, then retstarting and opening a new campaign. Just "skip the tutorial" on your new game (it offers this if you've already played it on another campaign) and everything proceeds fine from there.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Don't bother with the Steam version, just buy an activation key from Amazon and download from Uplay directly. I've done that and experienced no problems getting this running on Windows 10. 
You NEED mods to make this a genuinely enjoyable game however because SH5 was rushed out of the door and all support for the game was dropped very soon after release (f*ck you, Ubisoft).
I play with the Wolves of Steel megamod (follow the installation instructions carefully), d3d antilag (must have to avoid annoying mouse lag) and DBSM.
Modded up, I'm having a blast with this. It's basically Silent Hunter 3 with improved graphics and the ability to explore your entire boat from bow to stern. Nice blend of calm sailing, nail biting tension when engaged by enemy destroyers and genuine satisfaction when you pull off a perfect torpedo shot. 
Sad the devs were never given enough time to fully realise their vision - this could have been a truly fantastic simulation.
As it stands, with mods, it's pretty damn good.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I typically like sub sims and have several, including Silent Hunter II, III, IV and now V.   Silent Hunter III was by far the best of the series for several reasons and while Silent Hunter V had a bunch of potential, it really falls short even for the low price.

Overall, I have to say this thing ought to still be in beta.  It's got more bugs than a cheap hotel in a third world country and Ubisoft has done nothing to fix them.  They are just there and you're going to have to deal with it.  Some examples are:

1 - The hatch in the conning tower won't stay open on some missions.  This means that your crew is going to sufficate before you can end the mission and there's nothing you can do about it except restart the game as the last save point.

2 - When you restart from the last save point, you will be facing the "surviving" ships from the original encounter with the appropriate level of damage and flooding and the proper amount of torpedoes and main gun ammo.  However, sometimes (more often than not) you do lose everything you've accomplished during that particular mission.  So, you lose the tonnage and ammunition you sunk and expended before you killed off your crew by not being able to open the hatch.

3 - Depth charge damage seems to be constant regardless of location of enemy destroyers.  So, if you have to crash dive to avoid destroyers, even if there isn't a ship within 500 meters, you are going to take damage.  Which means if you are spotted by an enemy, you are automatically gonna die.

4 - Navigation is tricky.  If you run at full time compression, plan to run aground sometimes even when there's no ground to run into. 

5 - Setting the game parameters at the start of a campaign as "custom" settings is an exercise in futility.  No matter what you select and no matter what's showing in the menu, the game is going to play in one of the "standard" settings, usually "Medium".

6 - The TDC doesn't function correctly in that you cannot establish distance using the periscope.  For example, I knew the ship was 2300 meters away.  When I used the TDC to find range, it returned a value of 26445 meters.  But, I guess if you get to point blank range it will work that way.  (<---  attempt at sarcasm)

These are just a few bugs that I've found terribly annoying and, gamewise, fatal.

But there are other issues that just don't cut it.

1 - Movement around the sub is necessary to play the game.  I don't know about you, but if I were the captain of a U-Boat, I wouldn't run up and down the ladder and down the length of the ship to find out what's for freaking dinner tonight.  So, if you want to direct the sonar man, you have to go to his station and see what he will do for you at that time.
2 - Battle Stations are pretty much like any other day...  What's the point?
3 - The old axiom of "One ship, one torpedo" that made U-Boat aces during the war didn't play this game.  It typically takes 3 torpedoes to sink a modest freighter and sometimes more.  While I get it that not all ships would sink after one hit, a few did.  But certainly, after being whacked by 3 fish, a WW2 freighter would exhibit some degradation of performance.  Usually in this game it takes at least 3 hits and some deck gun work to bring a small freighter to a "dead in the water" state.
4 - If you are unfamiliar with this series, don't think the manual that comes with it is going to do you much good because it's pretty much worthless.  You will have to hunt and peck around YouTube to find tutorials done by well meaning individuals.  The few YouTubes I found made by Ubisoft were nothing short of shameless advertisements.

Let's talk about graphics...  Naw.  They really aren't that good.  But I guess they're acceptable.  

No matter where you turn, people who are playing this game have it loaded up with mods to make it playable.  But I still hear there are many problems.

I could go on but what's the point?  After 22 hours of frustration I've decided that I've had my pocket picked

Bottom line with this one...  Not good.  Wolves of the Pacific (SH IV) wasn't very good but it was better and more playable than this version.  

My recommendation is to let this one pass and wait for something better.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I didn't have a problem running the game, not sure why other people are having issues. The only mention of uPlay is when it synchronizes saves online.

As for the game itself:

For people looking for some casual submarine fun I do NOT recommend this game, cold waters is better for that kind of experience. SH5 has issues that require some extra effort to work around, it's by no means a plug and play game.

For people looking for a hardcore u-boat simulation experience this game is a must have, but not in its vanilla state. The vanilla experience is watered down in comparison to SH4, but there are a ton of mods available through subsim.com which make it a great successor to SH4, especially if you're sick of / not particularly enthralled by the restrictive interactions in SH4. You'll find yourself wandering throughout the entire length of your ship throughout each of your patrols and it really plays into a sense of immersion that no other game offers. If you don't want to worry about compiling a list of mods, installing them correctly etc I would recommend the Wolves of Steel modpack which introduces a ton of high quality mods which greatly enhance the immersion and overall experience, but at the cost of a somewhat steep learning curve. I've spent dozens of hours trying to master my use of the various instruments and measurements to compile a correct torpedo solution to the point where i can usually hit about half of all the torpedoes I fire, but I'm still constantly improving. Steam says i've spent 20 hours playing but I had to take the game offline for a couple mods which require some more extensive tweaking, I'd estimate my play time to be over 100 hours as of the time of this review and it inspired me to spend another 50 hours studying WWII submarine warfare and torpedo solutions to improve my tonnage each patrol. If you're a hardcore warfare sim fan who fancies the idea of diving to excessive depths to avoid depth charges, silently sneaking in among the ranks of a massive escorted convoy in the dead of night, or straining every last watt out of your batteries to move into a mined harbor unseen to take out Royal Navy battleships, then this is the game for you, and for $10 it's hard to go wrong.

Tip: in the campaign selection screen type "silentotto" to unlock all the missions. I haven't the patience to complete an entire mission just yet as each spans the course of a year or more in game time, and from what I've heard there's some issues with the game counting your progress in a particular mission to complete it so if you're looking for some variety after a few long patrols you can explore some of the other campaign missions, each having some unique challenges of their own.

review: It would be a great game if it were not for Ubisoft's poor support practices and it's selfishness in regards to always having to slave you to it's servers to play any of it's games.  The game needs an extra Ubisoft Laucher to run before it does and no surprise the launcher is itself buggy and usually isn't given log in priority on the Ubi servers.   So the launcher is buggy and won't start the game even it three months ago.  If it runs, there is a glitch in how the game advances after successful missions.    There's a glitch if you exceed a certain tonnage sank in one mission or spend too much time at sea.    Like many games the whole thing loads onto your PC, technically you shouldn't need to connect with Ubi at all, in fact the original pre-steam version didn't require hooking into the ubi servers, but this one does just so Ubi can track your use of your Ubi account.   Thing is its been 4 years since the last time Ubi payed anyone to work on support for this game or it's launcher.   Now apparently there are conflicts with the latest defender update and yadda yadda.   I suspect moreso that other sites comments about Ubi are closer to the truth, that Ubi is purposely not supporting anything more than a couple years old, and not wanting the launchers logged on it's servers when demand for server time is high from it's current games.  So between lack of support, glitches, and how bad a track record Ubi has for offering any game support for anything beyond it's latest moneymaker, I don't recommend this game at all.   Ubi has some of the greatest designers and creates awesome games, sadly the're games age about the worst on the market due to Ubi simply not wanting to support anything not brand spanking new and generating massive profits.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
PLEASE NOTE THIS IS NOT A WHOLE ENDORSEMENT OF THE GAME - MY RECOMENDATION IS CONDITONAL

Silent Hunter V is the last game in the WWII submarine simulator series. It's buggy, unstable, with odd design choices that take away some of the freedom of the past games. While being able to walk around your sub is cool, was it worth it at the cost of everything else? In my opinion, no it wasn't. Especially since on top of that the game is riddled with bugs and is generally unstable.

But why then would I be conditionally recomending it?

Simply put, The Wolves of Steel Modpack. Wolves of Steel compiles many mods (and makes some changes of its own) to make the game much more like the previous games in the series in terms of simulation (no more tonnage bar for example). Since it is a "full realism" modpack, playing with it can be extremely difficult and you'll most likely spend many hours watching YouTube tutorials on things such as target recognition, RAOFB use, using the TDC for manual torpedo firing, and more. It's not for everyone. On top of that, you do need to use some elbow grease to allow it to work with the Steam version. I installed the mods ages ago, only really started playing it recently, so I don't have much memory of that work but it couldn't have been too bad if I could get it to work.

If hardcore sub-sims appeal to you, then SH5 with the Wolves of Steel pack is simply put the best out there. So if you're into that, I can cautiously recomend the game (due to the unstable nature, and work involved in getting WoS working with the Steam version its still "cautious" for that group). If you're not into that at all, stay away.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Silent Hunter 5 is just another unfinished, rushed-out money-raker among hundreds of major titles we've seen over the last 5 years or so. Gaming industry standards have sunk to an all-time low, the rare exceptions are those games developed by individuals or partnerships who dedicate their lives to a single game/simulation, and listen their customers. It is now common place for a game to either be released unfinished and dumped, or unfinished on the basis that customers are are then held duty bound to throw more money at the shitty franchises to buy DLC which may or may not eventually transform the buggy half-arsed game into something enjoyable, let alone useable.

Terms such as "Sandbox" and "Open World" are meaninglessly bantered about, while there's usually nothing sandbox or open World about any of it. Your "deep and immersive experience in this totally sandbox open World" is nothing more than a numb-brained hand-holding exercise. Run down this path, the enemy will have a silly red icon floating above its head, whereupon you have to crouch like this behind that object and shoot it with that gun .. through that pissing rock.

Oh how close SH5 came to being one of THE most Sandbox and Open World RTS games of all time. What better platform could there be for stealth and strategy on a grand scale, than a Submarine simulation/game. I know, I know; what defines a simulation. I guess it's what you make of it in your own mind, with your own imagination.

Anyways, after 38 hours of not really going anywhere or doing anything much, beyond repeating the 'tutorial', I finally managed to transform SH5 into a gorgeous challenging immersive game. Realism and Graphics Mods to a final count of 35 via SubSim, in a very specific order. By God you Modders are worth their weight in Gold!

Bearing in mind the importance of "order" when it came to installing Mods, I then set about numbering them in the event that I would at some point need to reinstall them. At this point I Zipped them away, and uninstalled/deleted those Mods from the MODS folder created in the game's directory. This, in order to test reinstallation of the working Mods I'd put together.

Considering SH5 is an unfinished ditched product left to rot, Ubisoft have in place an utterly pathetic bull-headed protection mechanism that now won't even allow me to read the game directory. Installing Mods is out, obviously.

Silent Hunter 5 is now "LOCKED", and cannot be reinstalled. Ubisoft GET A F.....G GRIP! This is an unfinished discontinued buggy old game ffs.
Ubisoft are still churning out unfinished buggy shite, of the numb-brained hand-holding type I might add. They are a bloody disgrace, and are a sad indictment of many of today's gaming franchises.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Ok so i looked at this and i thought, man i used to love silent hunter 3 I'd Love to play that with better graphics and hopefully more stuff. If you to are thinking this do not buy this game. It looks so much fun right? I was even like well you have to walk around the ship thats abit annoying but i'll get used to. However after loading it up i quickly realised that the invert mouse function on this game does not exist, I know this is personel preference but if you use invert there is no way you can use normal i think it's a brain thing (i'm making that up). But anyways thats pretty standard for any game these days specially a game claiming to be a simulator, People actually look up by pulling there heads back. Well as you can see i'm quite salty about that (naval pun hur hur).


 There are some features that i liked and thought were really cool like the sonor and closing the doors to get better sound and things like that. however this game just wasn't fun or user friendly. You cannot swap quickly between your stations so thats means if your below deck when you spot a ship you have to run climb the ladders to the top, Actually know that you can climb from the bridge to the deck to find the gun. You cannot see the deck gun from the bridge only the AA gun at the back, So it took me like 20 minutes to work out where this ladder was and that yes indeed this sub is fitted with a deck a gun. I know some of you are thinking this is simulator thats what you do and if thats what you want then this is game for you. There is an officer to talk to that can send men out onto the gun and give them funky orders but well well that does that leave you doing staying below decks counting the potatoes.

 There are many mods that will fix this game and make it fun i'm sure but it's not on the workshop the mods didn't seem to hard to figure out but more hassle than i fancied. If i buy a game i dont really want to have to go the extra mile to make it fun. my advice is get Silent hunter 3 when it's on a super sales and live with the graphics and actually have fun

And its Crashes More than the Waves on Rhyl Pier

review:
Oh boy. Now, I love sub sims, I love world war II, I love pretty much everything that is in the theme of the game. It had promised so much, and I was expecting quite a lot. And, for the first part, ( Uplay was giving me a tough time, but you can get past it. ) it was a very good game. BUT. There are things that completely ruin this game.

A. Sometimes if you sink a ship, it doesn't count towards the mission. This is so frustrating, especially when you hunt a battleship.

B. Aircraft never attack. Though, you can never hit them, so... Meh.

C. Bugs, bugs, bugs, beside from A, it can completely ruin the game,

So, overall, if you want anything good, download hell's worth of mods. I, unfortunatley, can't, so this game is sorta destined to sit on the shelf and dust. But, of course, I've seen other people play it, and with mods, this can become what it promised to be. So save your money unless you know how to mod correctly ( And no, it isn't workshop compatable. That's why it's hard to install mods. ) I've played 99.5 hours. So. Final verdict?


WITHOUT MODS, VANILLA: 3/10

WITH MODS: 10/10


So either prepare yourself for a hell of a lot mod installing, or just don't buy the game. Vanilla, it's at least worth even 7.99 tops. Maybe 9.99, but that's just me.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
"How bad can it really be if I mod it heavily to fix all the problems?" I thought.

Even when modded (I'm using The Wolves of Steel megamod), this game is garbage. First of all, you can't properly mod the Steam version of the game. Secondly, the game tends to CTD at the beginning of patrols, or any time you've left a busy harbor. You have to save, quit, and reload the game to try to prevent it. You get skill points for crew. After upgrading a skill, it'll sometimes get "forgotten" and you will have lost a hard worked for skill point. You can only ever command the one U-boat: Type VII. When you get bored of walking up and down your boat a few times, you'll tire of never being able to trade in for another type. While the graphics are somewhat improved, the joy of better graphics is more than offset by the bugs you'll encounter. The only excitement besides stalking merchant ships is having warships zoom in from over the horizon and circle your position for hours despite everything you do to hide. While I was no fan of Silent Hunter IV, it's graphics were great. While Silent Hunter III is over a decade old, it still has better world depth and stability. The series is in desperate need of a new game, but this wasn't it. If you're in the mood to torpedo a ship, dust off a copy of SHIII. Save yourself the frustration and tears.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
[b] Before and during the Second world war, Germany was built, staffed and prepared to sail 1153 submarines. 15 foreign submarines had been requisitioned or captured, manned and commissioned. Total combat losses of the Kriegsmarine in the Second world war amounted to 766 submarines. The total losses of allied and neutral countries from German submarines made up, according to one, 2820 ships, on the other, more accurate, 2779 ships.	[/b]
[h1] Now about the game!	[/h1]
[i] "Quiet hunter" invites us to play a captain of a submarine and take part in the Second world war. Hunt we have in the Northern coast of Europe. This process is sometimes very tedious, and here comes to the aid of the ability to accelerate. From the previous parts of the game differs with improved graphics. Having the map open whilst in Periscope mode for example, is good. Alteration of course on map is simple, but great. Lots of great ideas have been ruined by a crappy mission tracking system imho. 

However, there are too many of those mission breaking issues that it makes it a huge waste of time to play. Games like this need patches, and Ubi seem to have given up on this game years ago. Too soon after it was released.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
This game, with freely available Mods installed from subsim.com, really brings home the tensions and flavor of operating a UBoat in WWII.  It is a Fantastic game (as you can tell from the number of hours I’ve played).   But be warned: this is way more than a "torpedo game".  You have to survive getting resupplied by sailing thru enemy blockades and patrols, learn to conserve torps by using a deck gun, learn to recharge your limited underwater batteries , learn how to navigate and compute target speeds and course angles, and learn various evasive maneuvers against the  threat of Airplanes, Destroyers, Sonar and Depth charges.  Some of these skills you learn by watching YouTube vids, some by asking others online, and some from the school of hard knocks.  In this regard you can Save and Reload your game to try different engagement scenarios, which is what I do till I perfect the best possible strategy.

Out of the box, the game can be played in a simplistic mode, and everybody starts there, but the real thrill comes from learning to take more control of your boat and the little things that make it work efficiently.  This is where the MODs come in.  They can enable things and actually completely transform the game into a serious UBoat simulation.  

Principle among skills you’ll need to learn is how to aim and angle a torpedo for real (and there are plenty of YouTube vids that cover this).  The torpedo has its own gyro compass and you can “program” it to turn in almost any direction except directly around and hit your own sub.  Mastering the TDC (Torpedo Data Computer) is key, and  its faithfully recreated in the game.    With it, in the midst of a convoy you can now target and fire at a number of different ships in seeming rapid fire, without turning your sub, as soon as the torpedo tubes are reloaded.

Watching your torpedoes propel forward toward their targets can be nerve wracking.  You can watch them zoom off toward their predicted target intercept locations, but sometimes you can't wait to verify a hit... because You’re too busy setting up the targeting solution for your next target while they're enroute.  Thank goodness, you can speed up, slow and even stop TIME, so you can work out what has to be done and in the right sequence.  There's some work involved here which develops into a Confident Skill.  The game becomes addictive then.  There's a big disappointment if some of your torpedos miss, a huge feeling of satisfaction when they all connect.

Can’t say enough about the game really.  If you’ve ever had a secret desire to run  your own Submarine, this is definitely it.

One last thing, the sea and sky simulations are really beautiful, and I sometimes I take a break and just “ride” my sub on deck and watch the clouds, sunsets, and storms.  Cameras can be moved anywhere, inside or outside the boat, in the sky, even underwater.   Some of the images you get look photo real.  Very very beautiful game. 

Hope this helps.

Original review:
Garbage. Dont waste your money.

I didnt have any problems getting the game to run (Windows 7 x64). Just logged in to my ucrap account and activated it, and it worked fine.

Then, After (trying to) play this trash for a while, i spent several hours researching & downloading mods to try to fix the COUNTLESS bugs and shortcomings on it. 
I then spent 13 hours (past the 2 hr steam refund time) testing mods.
Even the Stadimeter is broken FFS! even THAT needs mods to fix it.

14 (yes Fourteen) mods later, this thing is somehow usable (NOTE: "usable" is not the same as "fun" or "good"), but still nowhere near to SH4, and LIGHTYEARS away from SH3. 
Its more of a brainless "action" game, for people who think their head is only there to put on hats.

Shame on you Ubisoft, for releasing a product in this state.

Bottom line:
If you...
- Dont mind spending a few hours researching and downloading mods to make it usable
- Never played a SH game before
- Are NOT (repeat, NOT) interested in anything regarding a simulation but rather a brainless, unrealistic "action game"
- Are willing to sacrifice playability, realism, strategic play, historical accuracy for "eye candy"
- Think "Das Boot" is a brand of shoes
- Want to play with "helpers" on (e.g. automatic torpedo targeting)

You *might* find this thing... "Ok"

But if you want a decent uboat game/sim, Get Silent Hunter 3. Or at the very least, Silent hunter 4.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
This game had so much promise. You can get around the hell of Uplay, but the game is really really, really buggy. And the fact that most mods dont work with the steam version, your just sol. The game is visually beautiful, and there is something inertly great about being stealthy in the sea and then evaiding capture, but the bugs will have you ripping your hair out. Radar contacts sometimes dont work, and you'll be searching for multiple hours real time waiting to run into someone. AA gunners wont attack planes that are VERY relentless, let alone actually kill them. 6/10 times they wont even shoot at the plane when you have the gun armed, manned and ready; and when they do attack, 8/10 times even an experienced gunner wont bring them down.

I wanted to love this game so so much, but Ubisoft failed us. There are no updates to the game in over 5 years; the only "news" steam has on it was in 2010, and thats just them saying "hey we have this now". 

There is no explination of how to fire torpedos without manually entering eveything in. It took me a min to figure it out--online, not in game--and the way it was implimented was fail. You have numbers 1-3 on your projected torpedo trajectory, and you line up the numbers with the enemy ships numbers. Its really easier to just manually do it, but for new players both are very confusing and there is NO tutorial. Steam says in the description, "Now, every beginner can successfully command a sub free of confusion." That could not be any more of a lie. The tutorial is a joke, and you will actually have an icon at the top of your screen always telling you "use the mouse buttom to look around". That could just be a glitch i personally have, but either way it proves my points. In SH4, if you decided to not manually everything in, it was point and fire. Which if your a beginner, is fun and easy. It is not fun in SH5 for beginners, unless you take the time to learn manually doing everything.

This game could have been fantastic. And I do wish SH 6 would be made; but it cant be made by ubisoft. They will screw it up like they have since SH4. I have not played SH3, but many say it is the best one, so check that out first. The odd of anyone else making a SH game is slim to none, but one can dream.

Its really sad Ubisoft rushes everything (i wont even start a Assassins Creed rant) always pushing out the next new product. They put the product out, then once it stops selling, they literatly DO NOT CARE if the game gets buggy, at LEAST in regards to SH series. "Whats in if for us?" they ask themself. "Why update the game when no one will buy it from us?". Well, thats because you guys released a buggy half assed game and didn't put the love into it. Thats why I dont love it. If you guy just made ONE update to the game, and cleared up the rampent bugs, MANY people who play this game would recommend it to others. But alas, your Ubisoft, and you dont give a shit.

Sorry for putting words in your mouth Ubi, but you know its true.

Edit: I'd like to update this, though it is more directed towards Ubisoft. As aformentiond, I do enjoy SH4 better, it seems more thought out, but bugs are still very much present in earlier patch(s) of the game. After doing some research, v1.5 fixes an issues with magnetic impact toggle not working, which is in my opinion a major hinderence to stratigical gameplay (though not game breaking of course). 
But you should know that when purchasing SH4 from steam, you are only getting v1.3. In order to get v1.5, from steam, you need to also purchase the DLC U boat missions. This is ridiculous. I have never encountered a game where you need to purchase DLC in order to get a patch that applies to root game. Its really like they are trying to force our hand. I think the general point has gotten across. It definatly will be once you read the reviews for that game, everything advises you to not get it from Steam. Whats really sad, is that I'm actually very tempted to buy the DLC,  I want that update, because honestly this game series is the poster child for sub sims. They basically have a monopoly on it and look where that has taken us, the consumer. Is sub sim a nitch market? Yes. Although I would argue the genre is no more or less a nitch market then 4x games, or survival games; usually there needs to be market compitition for a genre to, for lack of a better word, get better. You need to try and build the game better then the other guy, but with the SH series there was pratically no compitition, which i believe let to SH5 sucking.

If only I appied the time I took to write this id apply to my school papers.
I'm also quite happy the one time I gave a shit to write a review for a game it actually becomes the most helpful, albeit negative review. I hope sometime down the line, I can change this to a recommendation.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I really tried to like this game but it was unplayable from the start. Right out of the gate it wouldn't even load because UPlay reported that some Ubisoft service was down. I finally worked around that by downloading UPlay from Ubi's website and launching it that way. The tutorial in this game is horrible -- it shows you how to shoot at surface targets that don't move. It doesn't enable control panels that you're supposed to use during the tutorial. At no point does it teach you about diving your submarine, how to operate submerged (i.e. running out of oxygen, depleting your batteries, etc). The first mission brief after the tutorial asked me to choose one of two missions and neither one was available to select on the map. After restarting the campaign a few times they finally showed up so I selected one where I needed to sink some merchant vessels. While on that mission I surfaced my submarine but the game thought I was still submerged (even though I'm walking around on deck). Because the game thinks I'm submerged my deck crew won't come up topside and my CO2 is continuing to accumulate. After sinking a few ships I check my mission status and find that it has not recorded any of my kills as progress towards the mission goal. 

I spent more time restarting, saving/loading, and searching forum posts for workarounds than I spent actually playing the game. This is not even an alpha game at this stage.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Silent Hunter is a long standing series and many say the best of the WW2 subsims. #5 is back in the u-boats. (#4 did US subs in the Pacific). The game play is familiar and excellent. The 3d ships are a joy to fight among. See below for a critical issue fix.

When the game first came out it was among the first to have must-have-internet and that time it was avoided by alot for that. That was years ago and constant internet connections are now very common. That was a factor in my waiting until now to play.

The crew intereaction for the time was innovative. Though now in 2016 with the same one line comments it seems a bit dated. Seems to lag when docked with the lighting and the people. This is on a good gaming rig of 2016. I could only imagine how buggy that would have been in the early years.

Not sure even if there are tutorials but Im sure there alot of guides and lets play now. Ive sub simmed alot so even managed to fuddle through a manual TDC (torpedo data computer). If you haven't done one of those before you definitely need to have a tutorial, guide or video watch.

The critical issue warning is that Steam and Uplay don't connect well. There are some guides and this is a quick one I did.
1) download and setup SH5
2) try playing and if you get a Uplay error thats their internet connection service
3) Goto Ubisoft, set up an account, download Uplay and install
4) Have uplay logged in when you next try to start SH5

Thats what worked for me.

On a scale of 1-10 id put things over all at a 6.5 compared to modern options. Still playable, fun and a neat setting.

Got it now as part of the Xmas sale and for an onsale game Id rate it 8 out of 10.  Very functional though the few hiccups tend to break my immersion at times.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Way too many issues out of the box.

Moving past the Uplay issue (Hint: install Uplay beforehand, have an account ready), which is annoying but not groundbreaking, I still can't recommend this game. On Win8.1 at least, had to uninstall every 2008 edition of Microsoft Visual C++ Redistributable just to load past the menu, as it would just crash upon loading into the game. I don't know why it was that specifically, but thanks for the heads up, internet. Do your research before calling it a day.

The vanilla game itself feels considerably rushed. Fewer subs, poor AI, graphical glitches out the wazoo.

Sure, it looks nice, but the changes from previous games are, at least initially, disorienting if you're coming from an earlier game. It might seem petty, but remapping all the controls to fit in the finnicky 'walk around the sub' mechanic (which is admittedly a nice touch) screwed with my muscle memory for at least an hour. Managing to get my sub glitched inside a larger ship thanks to time compression didn't help either.

It does a couple of things right for casual players though. Having a toggleable map might take away some of the challenge, but it does wonders for situational awareness. Crew management is, for the most part, a non-entity, which for me personally is a... semi-welcome change from SH3, but again removes a lot of the challenge. Instead of rapidly throwing crew members from room to room to min-max their rapidly decreasing energy in SH3, we get running around the submarine talking to the senior officers. At least, I think that's the intention, but you never seem like you're forced to do it. Morale doesn't increase or decrease at a noticeable rate, except when you botch a conversation for the first time.

AI crewmen can't be set to fire the deck gun or AA gun without you expressly selecting a target, which might seem fine for ships, but is a pain in the arse when trying to pick out planes. And yes, I know sneaking away from the planes is the preferred solution, but if you're going to give us the option, Ubisoft, why make it so darn difficult?

Honestly, some of my gripes would probably fade away over time, but I don't believe Silent Hunter 5 is a product worth investing into, especially not for the asking price. If you can get Silent Hunter 3 or 4, do that.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I was leary about buying this, but it's cheap and I figured what the hell. My experience pretty much went like this: installed the game, then it installed uPlay, which crashed repeated. Found solution was to uninstall uPlay and then re-install it. Okay, then it made it to the game and I started a new career. Went through the minimalist tutorial and then discovered the missions are glitched and won't show up on the first attempt at the career. So you have to restart it and skip the tutorial. Fine. I start out in port on a new patrol and realize there's a glitch where your periscope always starts raised. This basically continues... you play a while, and find another glaring bug or the game crashes.

On the brightside, it looks pretty and is fun in an arcady way when it works, but it should never be considered a simulator. I waltzed into Scapa Flow totally surfaced and sank several ships without the British even showing the slightest sense of alarm. I almost deckgunned a battleship to death there and probably would have been successful... but the game CTD'd before I got the chance. The AI just sucks. The only chance of death in this game is from running aground or hitting another ship in time compression.

Furthermore, what really annoys me are the fundamental flaws with this game that fancies itself a simulator. For instance, when I started out, realizing I hadn't adjusted the difficulty and had unlimited fuel, I decided to just go on a joyride. I'll leave Kiel and head up over the north pole and over to Alaska and down the coast of Asia, I thought. Then I realized the map is horribly flawed and the developers apparently believe the world is a cylinder and not the oblate spheroid I was taught about growing up. That meant my "shortcut" over the pole wasn't going to be so short. This also means it reduces the range your sub has from what it would be in reality. For something where maritime navigation is fundamental to the game, this is just plain unforgivable, especially when every other maritime or aviation sim out there treats this as essential for proper navigation. Actually, there are a lot of unforgiveable aspects of this game. Too many to mention in just one review (which is probably why there are so many negative reviews).

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Remember everything that you loved about the Silent Hunter series?  Good, hold on to those memories, because this game has got none of that going on.

In a sad attempt to make things more "cinematic" they force you into a first person mode through the entire game.  Want to check your map?  Walk to the map room.  Want to check periscope?  Walk over to the periscope and push a bunch of buttons.  Want to make a lightning fast surface raid on an unescorted convoy? Tell helm to surface, manually lower periscope, climb up two ladders through the conning tower, open the hatch, climb out (to find two other guys already up there, somehow), climb back down the outside of the tower, walk over to the deck gun and manually fire it.  Uh oh, an enemy destroyer appeared?  Need to crash dive?  Walk back to the tower, climb up the outside... yeah, you get the picture.

You're not the captain.  You're a crewman.  In fact, you are [i]every[/i] crewman.  You are running that entire submarine yourself in a room full of animatronic mannequins.  It's like being on the wrong end of some u-boat creepypasta, the eternally damned captain on a ship full of ghosts or something, doomed to repeat the same dull sequence of needless actions for eternity.

This series peaked at 3, nose dived at 4, and now at 5 has finally come to rest at the bottom.  The graphics have never been better and the gameplay has never been worse.  I could go into the details on the bugs, the incomplete features, the downright wrongness of so many of the games dates & stats, but I don't feel like writing another sixty paragraphs nor should I have to.  Even if it [i]wasn't[/i] your typical Ubisoft pile of spaghetti code it would still be a thumbs down just for having such flaccid gameplay to begin with.

I downloaded some free mods from subsim and I played for about 6 hours straight and had a blast.  I haven't played this series since the original so I can't comment on those ones but I think this game is great.  This is not an arcade game. name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Let me just preface this by saying that Ubisoft are idiots for forcing their crappy DRM into a Steam release. Delete the Ubisoft Game Launcher crap that it installs on your computer because it's not going to work. You need to go to download UPlay instead and maybe even do a couple other things. As long as you're willing to potentially spend about 20-30 minutes on google trying to figure out how the hell to get it to work it's a pretty good game.

Sinking enemy ships with manual targeting on High Realism/Full Realism is very gratifying once you figure out how to do it. The new UI isn't that great and is a bit of a pain to get used to but being able to walk around your submarine and interact with things directly is pretty neat. If you're a stickler for historical accuracy there's a website called SubSim that carries a lot of mods that do things like improve the game's graphics and give the Germans historically accurate flags. Y'know since swastikas are apparently illegal in some places.

All in all pretty decent game. There are worse ways to spend $10.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
This game is so disappointing after the previous installments. Visually it is great. Sound, great. The basic missions, good. But the mechanics of the game, the UI layout, the controls are just so sloppy and bad that it makes handling the sub so tedious and frustrating. There are annoying bugs as well and the gimmick of walking around as a captain in the 1st perspective, while very welcomed in theory, can get annoying when trying to desperately descend and ascend ladders. 

I would only recommend this game if you are prepared to mod the hell out of it. Luckily there is a very big and dedicated modding community. With the mods, yes, this game is worth playing. But playing it plain will just make you realize how much you're missing SH 3 and 4.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
NEVER buy this game, it's unfinished, crappy, unworking, glitchy and they officially stated on the forums that they NEVER will fix it, they abandoned it. So WHY does steam sell this piece of crap when the game is unfinished? What the f**k!

Slightly longer rant:
What a piece of elephant dung. This is the last time I ever buy a game from UBI soft ever. They have done these sorts of stunts before and this was the last straw, go join Electronic Arts in the shame corner. I bought this game on steam because I really loved Silent Hunter 3 (and I’m quite good at it if I might brag). Well, the similarities between Silent Hunter 3 and 5 is they both are games about submarines. And that’s about it. Silent Hunter 5 is a bug infested frustrating random event generator of horror. The graphics are great, sure. When it’s not flickering out of existence, casting opposite shadows randomly when moving the camera around and that sort of stuff. The sound isn’t much better. You move about the ship and somehow they managed to miss the most common bug of ones character making an insane stepdance sound. The lord of the fucking riverdance occuring neatly on a sine curve every minute.

And hey, it’s a submarine game. I know, let’s man the deckgun to shoot down THE HOVERING FUCKING MERCHANTS flying around in the air. Oh, I forgot. I can’t man the deckgun because IT’S BUGGED! What a ripoff. There is no way they missed bugs as these in these numbers. It was an intentional scam. But I must admit some hilarity ensued when my crew hanged around on top of my submarine keeping lookout while I was submerged. Nothing beats that Pirates of the Caribbean feeling. And then it keeps going with integral game mechanics that randomly stops working, stuff disappearing between saves, etc.

To sum it up the state of this game is somewhere in pre-alpha. And hey, UBI soft also installs some hefty DRM for you to make sure you enjoy this experience to the max. Sometimes crackers fixes the bugs for the lols when they crack copy protections. I wonder if they have done the same with this game? Maybe UBI-soft should ask them for help.

I gave up and concluded that surely there must have been some hickup in the patching and went to the official forums to get the latest patch. Lo and behold, UBI soft came out on the forums and officially stated thay they ABANDONED THE GAME. They will never fix it. This game came out in 2010. Why is Steam selling this 2013?

Silent Hunter 4 was also a disappointment but at least it wasn’t so buggy as to be classified as a scam.

My advice to you who wants to play a good submarine simulator. Go get Silent Hunter 3 instead. Install Grey Wolves pack and all the mods. Have realism on max. It’s really good. Enjoy.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I've been a HUGE Silent Hunter fan. Even played the orignal 2D game. However no matter how much I want to I can not recommend this game to anyone. I have modded this game front and back and it doesn't matter. This is arguably the most immersive silent hunter game however the immersion is contantly broken when the bugs come out and ruin just about everything. Want to try and enjoy the game? Well your going to have to download a MINIMUM of 10 mods just to get the game into a playable state. The only thing this game does right is the graphics can be pretty amazing. However you can get the same graphics with SH4 and have a polished and enjoyable experience.

Casual players should definitely stay away as there is no more "auto-lock" the only way you can score hits now is if you learn how to do it manually (which is actually harder than SH3/SH4 as they removed the useful interfaces) or you just start spamming all your torpedos and hope one hits. 

The community is amazing and they always have been however even the hardcore guys like Subsim are having a hard time justifying playing this game over SH4 and SH3 which are both without a doubt much better and polished games. The devs have completely abandoned this game as well so there is no hope that it will ever be fixed. Been about 5 years since release and still a heap of trash even with all the community support. 

Run silent, Run deep around this game.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic, isn't a terrible game by any means. It can be quite fun at times and exciting. Unfortunately, the glitches and bugs out weigh any fun this game will give to you. With the corrupted save files, ghost ships etc. But, if these were the only problems with the game, hell, I'd say give it a shot. Alas, it is not. And the problem this game suffers from, is not the game itself, no no, It's the beautiful, glorious, riveting Ubisoft. 

You've just installed the game. You hit the play button of course! Time for some fun, you'll give the game a shot. A few minutes later Uplay shows up to tell you that, "Hi! You need to create another account! Along with the millions and millions of other online accounts you already have! Ha ha!" So, begrudgingly, you decide to make another damned account. Twenty minutes later you're account is set up. Perfect. You've given them the product key and you're ready to play.  


Heck, maybe you even liked the game a bit. But a few months pass, you haven't played the game in a while, you uninstalled it because it was taking up room, and you thought, "I'll reinstall it if I ever want to play it again." So you get this odd urge to play a game on the high seas, sinking shipping and what not. You click the install button, wait a few, and you're off! Or, so you think. Uplay comes back and says, "Hi again! Please activate you're product by entering your product key to play!" Finding this strange since you already activated it once, you curiously do so. Copy and paste, and you're done. You reach for the OK button, you click. What happens? "Sorry asshole! This key is already being used by another account! Please contact our AMAAAAAAAZING customer support for more information! Ha ha!" 




~Ubisoft 
"Actually fuck you." 
1986-2014~




P.S Get Silent Hunter 3.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I do recommend this game but with a MAJOR Caveat. It Can be a pain in the butt to implement.

I run windows 8.1 on a pretty powerful gaming PC 

Bought this game as part of a package deal with SH3 & SH4  Both of those work great right after downloading.  SH5 can be a dud. It REQUIRES an internet update program be running when you launch the game (at least once) That program has caused problems for countless people. (Really, I didn't count them!) 

I've watched the game in you-tube let's play videos and would really love to try it but so far all attempts at remediating the problems (even following long checklists on subsim.com) have given me no joy. 


I did FINALLY get the game to play. All I had to do was...

Report problem to Ubisoft and plead for help there and in the subsim.com boards, then...

1) Uninstall the Ubisoft Game Launcher from the Control Panel
2) Go to Program (x86) folder and deleted the Ubisoft Folder containing the Game Launcher Folder
3) Run Registry Cleaner & Reboot the system
4) Down-load a new Game Launcher from here:
http://forums.ubi.com/showthread.php/675733-Ubisoft-Game-Launcher-Download-Forums
5) Reinstall the Game launcher
6) Go to the Game Launcher folder & Open the folder and go to the 1st Game Launcher .exe application icon and run as administrator (disabling the proxy option)
7) Enter my validation code and got an ubisoft screen with Play Silent Hunter 5 prominently featured in the center .

8) Go to the Steam/SteamApps/Common/SilentHunter5 folder and 
Rightclick on SH5.exe then choose Compatibilty Tab then set the X on "Disable scaling on High DPI) worked like a dream. (just for good measure I also ticked the box about "run as admin")

Easy Peasy! Now why didn't I do that right the first time?

As to the game-play itself. Life aboard a submarine was tough. This game simulates that. Many may find gameplay to be repetetive at points and at other otherpoints very uneventful. If you're like me you may get just as much enjoyment out of watching others play (hopefully with the boring bits cut out) as from playing it yourself.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
If you buy this game, be prepared to fight two wars at once; one against the British and the other against the game controls and interface. The latter seems to pose the greatest threat.  Silent Hunter 5 reeks of a game that has been rushed out the door in order to meet a deadline. The game is no more finished than an early beta release, but is being sold as a completed project. That's unfortunate because this game could have been a gem.

Missing are some of the basic controls you would expect in a submarine simulator. For example, you have no way of checking your depth under keel. This remarkable oversight would have sent every sub in WWII to the bottom, literally. There is no way to adjust game controls such as mouse tracking speed. This is incredibly frustrating since the tracking speed while in first person is incredibly slow. 

The AI is unpredictable and often just plain stupid. The safety of your home port is the most dangerous place in the game, as friendly ships will slam right into you with no attempt to avert the disaster. This is especially prevalant while time warping. You have to always save the game before time warping, otherwise you will find you ship destroyed due to the game's failure to pull you out of time warp when there is a danger (as it is supposed to do). Once my time warp was ended with the crew screaming about damage that had been sustained to the ship. That's not unusual. That happens all the time when the game fails stop time warping. However, what was most interesting was the fact that my boat was inexplicably on dry land, well inland.   

The graphics are, at times, absolutely gorgeous. It's to the point where I think the developers spent way too much time working on the graphics and didn't have enough time left to work on the interface. 

Fortunately, there is a modding community out there working on the countless bugs. If you choose to buy this game, you will need them. I'm just sad that this game, which is actually fun and interesting, was shoved out the door for the sake of a quick buck. I would only recommend this game to hardcore submarine or WWII enthusiasts who don't mind tweaking and modding the game to get it working halfway decently.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I loved the Silent Hunter series, and I was excited to play this title when I first heard of it. Sadly, this game does NOT live up to the high standards set by its predecessors.

The main flaws include the following:
Uplay DRM to play the game.
Game-breaking bugs and flaws that will sometimes prevent you from even progressing, or on occasion, will reset your progress to a much earlier point.
-Examples of this include:
  -When opening the periscope view the game instantly crashes to desktop with no crash dump, report, or warning.
  -Some enemy units will disappear in full view of the player without explanation or warning.
  -Certain story objectives are not acomplishable due to the game not tallying kills properly.

The main reasons to enjoy your time in this game:
All gamebreaking bugs aside, the game looks beautiful, and is a good platform for mods (assuming you are allowed to, I wouldn't know as I have only played the stock game).
The mechanics from the old Silent Hunter games are somewhat present, but the game is much easier than it's predecessors.

Overall:
Don't buy this game, buy Silent Hunter IV or Silent Hunter III

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review: The game runs fine with no modding for me, but I just can't enjoy it because of the bugs and design flaws  in the game.  The most frustrating one for me is the time compression which will get stuck and slam you into port regardless if you have travel mode set or not.  The other major glitch is that the game does not record kills properly so you can't achieve mission objectives and you "fail" said objectives.  There is a major glitch after the second campaign (if you fail it) which prevents you from continuing onward unless you restart the game and reload.  There are just too many bugs.  The game itself is missing elements like complete crew promotion and command and control within the sub.  As captain you have to personally man the deck gun, flak gun, etc. despite issuing orders to your officers.  This game is graphically pretty, but it lacks the substance of SH3.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Altantic; a great game for people who are fans of World War 2 and simulators. This game gives you realistic control of a German U-Boat in the start of World War Two, in late 1939. Your goal is to complete missions by sinking Merchant ships coming from the Americas, and other countries. Ships can travel in large groups and are sometimes escorted by British Detroyers, who will hunt you down and try to kill you. 

My personal experiences with the game are great. I do not know what other people are talking about with constant crashes. I have not had a crash to date, and also bugs, not a thing that happens to much. All games have bugs, this game has more than average bugs, but they are not severe. I have not installed any mods (mostly because I don't know where to find them), but I am guessing this is helping the fact that I havn't crashed yet. My computer is not to great. Is is not a laptop, but it is a decent step up. I run on Low/Medium graphics, but the game still doesn't look too bad. On a scale of 1/10, i give it a 9. It has bugs, it isn't perfect, and may be the only sim game that doesn't crash (for me at least).

The bugs will rear their ugly heads from time to time, most are merely amusing. Like the torpedo explosions that send ships flying hundreds of yards vertically upwards. name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
I love this game. I hate this game.

This game will RANDOMLY crash to desktop. Hours after dealing with this POS you will goto "subsim" forums. There the veterans have common knowledge of how to avoid crashes...ie, restart game 3-4 times during a patrol. Save all the time. Never save over a save. Never save with AI rendered. Never save near a sub. Don't get on the hydrophone with a sub near by. - STILL, you randomly crash to desktop. This games loading time is up there with CIV5...I kept on going though because I REALLY liked the game and put up with the random crash nonsense as well as minor in-game bugs...

Then you realize that there is a group of very dedicated modders (via subsim forums). You check out some pictures, you watch some videos, you get excited.

You realize that out of the multi-step process to mod the game YOU CANNOT MOD THE STEAM VERSION.

You buy a DL version or DVD version via ebay or amazon. LETS MOD! The game will be 9000 times more epic and not crash all the time weeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee!

WRONG.

After about 15 hours, I learned the ins and outs of modding SH5. I tried 2 different popular megamods. Random crashes STILL. Also it should be noted I have never had a worse modding experience. There is tons of information on subsim forums. I spent hours reading into the matter. I followed guides to the T. The game is a serious POS. It is still a POS modded. No offense to the modders of course. You can't make gold out of shit right?

ALSO NOTE: On steam you are buying the "gold edition" - HOWEVER since I purchased via amazon there is OBVIOUSLY different/more content. Conclusion; STEAM GOLD EDITION IS NOT REALLY GOLD EDITION. 

This game is cheap. Go ahead. You'll get your money out of it in gameplay hours.

The amount of time I have wasted though is not worth it in the end. 

UBI sucks. 

I hope a new Uboat sim is created, pray to your gods.

 If you're not up for that, you're not prepared to be a successful submarine sim captain.  All this game will give you is grief. 

On the other hand, if you take the time and install the mods (which is more fun than you might think), you're going to be rewarded with an experience so memorable and immersive you'll be hard pressed to find the same sort of satisfaction in other games.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
[u]In nutshell[/u]
Silent hunter 4 is better (buggy) investment, but do not buy steam version of that either. Community has given their best shot to fix what Ubisoft couldn't on that also. Many still say that SH3 is the best choise but personally I have not tried that so can't tell. Fact however is that silent hunters in general are still the best subsims out there untill some better game developer takes over.

[u]Why?[/u]
- Buggy game which Ubisoft won't update and/or fix. Even some instruments are broken. With or without mods it will crash to desktop in some point and most likely ruin your mission while doing that.
- New interactions with crew are a joke and they have removed crew experience system from this version.
- U can move "freely" inside the sub in 1st person view, but it is like walking in a pipe and after a while you get really annoyed to the fact that they guy isn't going where u want him to go and end up using "SH4"-style quick shortcuts again. In other words - useless feature which pretty much was the thing they used to sell SH5 and all it gives you is more bugs.
- Horribly slow mod loader system.
- Requires Uplay which is waste of your computer resources.

+ Great modding community which has added so much content and eyecandy to the game. They have even created some unofficial bug patches to the game, but they wont work with steam version and still won't fix the game entirely. SH4 has wide variety of mods also and IMO they are even better than those available for SH5.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review: This game is great, excellent graphics and very smooth playing. The only problem I have run into is the targetting system for your torpedoes, they dont always go where you want them too. One drawback is running out of torpedoes, just because these old subs did not carry very many, so when you run out, you must head back to your base and refit because so far no other german ship will refit you in the open waters. So depending on where you are, it could be a very long trek to refit. But there is a feature that lets you speed up the game and does cut down on your travel time, but when you do this the enemies can pop up right in front of you and next thing you know you are getting blown up, been there done that lol. So whatever you do, do not turn up the speed of the game and walk away to have a cigarette or eat dinner, chances are you will not survive. Also, keep checking with your dive officer of the depth because I have dove and hit the bottom and was killed. The minimum specs on this game is a little higher than what is needed, my first shot playing was with a core2duo 1.8ghz, geforce 9500gt, win 7, 3 gigs of ddr2 ram. The game played just fine with that setup. I recently upgraded my processor to a core2duo 2.5ghz and went up to 4 gigs of ddr2 ram, the rest stayed the same, and it did not make any difference in game play, still played smoothly. If you like sub games you will love this one.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
Unlike the brilliant Silent Hunter 3 and 4, Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic suffers from an identity crisis. One that holds back what could've been a fun game. I went into this expecting something inferior to 3 and 4 which, realistically, you can't top. The problem with this game is that it tries to do two things and fails at both. 1.) It tries to be a Sub Sim and 2.) It tries to involve the player more in the setting. It fails at both.

The game takes a new approach to the series and allows you to wander the vessel as you please. This could've worked amazingly well to sucking you into the experience, but any and all effort to do that is squandered. It's painfully clear that Ubisoft Romainia had Das Boot playing on a loop through out the entire development process. It could've been done, but it never is. While you can interact with the crew, never once do you get anything out of it other than gameplay choices. Not once do you hear your young radio officer mention his wife back in Munich or some where and how he misses her. This could've been a pretty artfully crafted experience in that regard, offering a less than jingoistic view of war that doesn't hit you over the head with its simplicity. But, that's never done. Turning Silent Hunter 5 into an artistically crafted game could've been done and, sure the core audience would've been alienated and upset but I could see them coming around and enjoying the experience either way.

Now, more damning still is the sub-sim aspect of the game: It's gone. Lazy is an understatement. Gone is the more realistic area based damage model that represented actual damage and, in its place, we have healthbars. Yes, health bars. Ships tend to take an obnoxious amount of damage before sinking. In addition, everything is dumbed down and automated. They took the sim out of the game all together. With that additional simplicity, surely that must mean more content given how much easier it would be to add boats. Right? Maybe even some wild stuff, like Seehunds? No, you got the Type VII. You get 3 flavors of Type VII. Now, don't get me wrong: the Type VII is the iconic boat of the U boat flotillas, but that's essentially one single play style. There's no tiny but nimble Type II that offers you a unique challenge with its strengths and weaknesses. There isn't a wonderfully obselete boat like the S-18 and S-42 that can given you the enjoyment of having to work around its short-comings. There isn't even the satisfaction of using the badass incarnate of a Balao or Type XXI. You have 3 variants of Type VII and that's it. On top of that, the game is lazily put together with men staying on conning towers as subs dive and even running on the decks of ships as if nothing happened while they're slipping past the 50m maker in depth after being sunk.

So, in short, it's a game that tries to dumb down Silent Hunter without adding in anything else.

In the end, of the 3 games on modern systems, Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic is the weakest. Sure, it looks great, but that's it. It's actually a lot like dating. Silent Hunter 5 is a model. Sure, they look great, but they aren't very fulfillling. They don't offer much other than looks. Silent Hunter 3 and 4 might not beat it there, but they'll stimulate you so much better. You'll find them to be infinitely better companions. It's not a necessarily bad games, but it suffers from not even half-realized ideas.

It will not endear itself to fans of the series all that much. Sure, some will pick it up on sale and mod it. That would make it decent, but the core game itself is just poorly executed. Fans of the series and subsims will leave due to the lack of depth when compared to the previous. Newcomers might pass on it due to the lack of excitement. Despite its forsaking of its roots, it still possesses that slow and calculating tension that Silent Hunter does so well. This will bore most others.

In the end, I can't recommend Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic. It's half-realized and lazy. Those looking for Sub simming goodness need to get 3 and 4.

I don't blame them; no one likes to be repeatedly reminded about their shoddy workmanship.  On the other hand, they shouldn't be asking money for it either.

Thanks to insanely die-hard fans of the series, there are numerous mods available online to improve the game.  Still, you shouldn't have to clean up someone else's mess.  Stick to SH 3 and 4, two really nice games for this genre.

When someone asks me about my favorite RPG I tell them that Silent Hunter 5 is my fave. Why? I mean it isn't wizards and warriors running around. But hey your crew does kind of level up. You do get phat lewtz and new gear. But the main reason I say I enjoyed this game the most as an RPG is that when you play it you can really dive into the idea of actually being a submarine captain. How so? Well you can play it entirely from the first person, so you can really immerse yourself in the situation. I've found myself more involved with my crew and my current situation in a game of SH5 than any of the big studio rpgs. I mean sure I couldn't walk around the ship and then romance each crew member after a mission, but hey you still start to project things onto them by the end of a campaign.

Anyways, pretty great game.

name: Silent Hunter 5: Battle of the Atlantic
review:
This is an excellent submarine simulator, but being an excellent simulator is always a two-edged sword.

Combat is fun and wel modelled, and the graphics are pretty. Wreaking havoc amongst enemy convoys is incredibly satisying, but getting to the battle in the first place is a very tedious process. The game does include a time dilation feature, but the autopilot will have serious difficulty navigating at higher speeds, unless you're in deep open water. This game will appeal to hardcore simulator buffs, but if you're turned off by the idea of spending hours just finding something to shoot, this is not the game for you.

Also, this game requires a internet connection and the Ubisoft online launcher to play. When I buy a game through Steam, I already have a layer of internet DRM. I don't appreciate it when a title requires me to install a second internet DRM launcher to play it.

At the end of the day, while I enjoyed wrecking convoys, the time it took to find and intercept the convoys plus the extra Ubisoft DRM has prevented me from picking the game up again after my initial binge.
